segments.text: Last story because don't get as many opportunities
that we'd like to tell Palmer Lanco story.
Just by way of introduction,
I've been given very strong message to keep it short.
So I will just, the key themes that I'd like to leave you
with from, from my perspective, um, firstly is
around financial performance.
It is, it is numbers one, two, and three.
In our focus, we have to be, we have
to earn our right to do other things.
And, and that's based around operational excellence
and financial performance.
And we take that really seriously.
Uh, this year we delivered in a net operating profit
or last year, net operating profit of $49 million.
And our forecast for 2026 is between 80 and $90 million.
Um, we consider that a significant turnaround.
Not all of that is based around protein prices
and significant, the not a significant increase
between last year and this year.
But our operational performance underpins that.
Um, uh, our performance on farm livestock
and land management has been exemplary in our view.
Uh, we've done managed
to achieve emissions reductions, freshwater improvements.
We continue with that sort of license top rate stuff
that we think is really important work in the East coast,
try to deal and help with significant dislocation problems
with changing climate.
Uh, on the East Coast where we have a significant
investment, uh, we continue to invest in value
beyond pure profit.
Still play a role around, uh,
private sector leadership and innovation.
Um, but it's all predicated now on value accretion.
So we won't do anything for the sake
of doing the right thing,
but we do it where there's a, a net benefit to our p
and l, uh, we'll at least no cost to our p and l, uh,
and where there's a, a social benefit as well.
And that's a bit of a, uh, easing off of
where we would've been in previous years.
But, uh, we think that's the appropriate balance
between economic outputs and, uh,
and doing the right thing at the system level.
We have, um, some, um, interesting
and challenging conversations around, uh, ownership changes,
as you will have heard in the press.
Um, and we spend a lot of time
focusing on our long-term relationships with Iwi as a,
as a repository for the Crowns, uh,
treaty settlement program, uh,
in farm in farmland particularly.
Um, and we spend quite a bit of time
positioning our portfolio in, in respect of what we would do
for, for our farming systems if those farms went out
of our system and into, into alternative ownership.
Uh, but that's just one of the limitations
and realities of the portfolio that we have In terms
of the ownership position, the crown,
we tightly respect the crown has the right
to make a decision, uh,
around ownership or change of ownership.
Uh, our job is to give a best sense of what
that should look like, preserve the value that we believe
that farming brings to the farming system,
and we continue to prosecute that, um,
and make sure that we fully understand
what we might be giving away at the first instance if we,
uh, were not able, um, to retain this asset in the way.
It's, um, so thanks very much for that, this opportunity.
We look forward in the course of today to be able to
fill in any gaps that you might have.
Um, and, um, and,
and just in closing, really it is important that
across government, that the, the position of LandCorp
and the system, at least for me, is really well understood.
It's a critical piece of the system, which I think
we would miss if it was gone.
And so we need to fight to understand it at the very least.
And, and it's why committee such as this is so important.
Thank you, uh, Joe,
Thank you. Um,
I've got lots of questions today, but um,

in this fabulous venue.

So it's a, so the question for Wills and,
and Mark here, you know,
if you're talking about a dairy farm, so
we average three 30 across the board
and we, we aspire to be 400 kilo milk solid farms by date X.
Um, and we want appreciate, appreciated livestock gains
of sort of 1.75 to two kilos a day
for anything over 18 months through to 22 months.
Those are the kind of things I think
you're talking about, aren't you?
And, and you seem to maintain that, that,
that there are limitations to that.
Is it then a case of, of, you know, the portfolio is
so broad, so wide
and with different land classes across the board that
by its very nature in a nature based system
is where it's so difficult?
Yeah. That, that, that's exactly true.
But remember, we've got this not by,
this is not a deliberate portfolio
that anyone's put together.
We've, we've inherited this by t of accident lands
and survey and all that great rich history of New Zealand.
Mm. So whatever you might describe
that portfolio, we are optimizing it.
And that's a really good, that's a, none
of this has really cherry picked prime land.
What we've demonstrated is New Zealand's Inc's capability
to turn pro average land into quite
a high performing entity.
That should be an exemplar
for all farming systems in my view.
We, we, we are happy to democratize it.
We are not competition.
We don't compete with our local farmers.
I know visually it looks like that sometimes,
but actually that's not where our books are opened.
We've board has made it very clear we
democratize all our data.
Anyone farmer in New Zealand can get access to it.
And we think that's a role that should be tested at least
before we give up on it.
So, um, I, I think you are, you are,
you are, you, you are right.
But we've got what we've got. We can't sell anything.
We can't buy anything. Mm-hmm.
So an exercise of getting, making the most of
what you've got is what we've done pretty well, I think.
Well these guys have anyway. Mm-hmm.
Thank you for that, Steve.
Mine in a sense is make it quick an extension on that.
It's, uh, I, I would say that your place is one
of innovation, um, demonstrating new land use,
being something of an experimental space, as well as proving
what production or productivity gains can be achieved
through different methods and different land use.
Animal welfare is thing you just mentioned
before, um, an issue of, uh, significant concern
to the New Zealand public and,
and the international community.
I've seen in the past some terrible images of, uh,
the impact of winter grazing
and some of your Southland farms.
Um, I wonder if you can reflect on the past season
what animal welfare issues you had
and whether you have considered
what other land use there could be for those farms
that you currently haven't went
to Intensive winter grazing in Southland.
Look, I think, uh, every year you get thrown challenges,
um, be it climate, be it, um, you know, disease
or whatever it might be within farming systems.
So we are trying to build a resilient, uh,
system design within all of our farming systems
to make sure that we endure those.
And, you know, we invest in that, um, animal house spend 7%
above, uh, industry.
Um, which we've gotta work on.
'cause we've gotta find a balance there. We don't wanna gold
plate it, but, uh, most certainly, you know,
we wanna make sure that our,
our welfare rates are best in class
and we work hard on those every day as a team.
I think your question, uh, your second question around are,
we've got the right systems in the right places
at the moment, Steve, we've been
about normalizing the business.
So dealing with what we've got, getting the right to be able
to look at divers, diversifying it to different, uh,
land use classes or designs.
So we've been firmly going back to core basics, um,
employing the traditional, uh, uh, simplification
of systems focused on cropping
and all that sort of thing to be able to enable us
to leverage the spring grass curve,
which we haven't been able to do historically
because we haven't had the grazing capacity held on farm
through winter to take advantage of that.
So we've done that now, now it's about, okay, well
how do we keep continuing to challenge our system design
to make sure that we can continue the productivity,
but also manage our cost structure to be more enduring.
We've got a vast area of land
that doesn't prevent us from going into other things
like horticulture in different areas.
Yeah. But it's about what we can extend at scale
to make sure that we are still maintaining profitability
while not, um, you know, don't do anything too drastic.
I wouldn't say that we've got the answer yet.
We, we've, you know, two and a half years into a,
a five year turnaround effectively.
Yeah. And, uh, until we've normalized
and we've got absolute focus on
what our expectation is today to change,
it would be difficult, um, for our team more than anyone.
But that's not to say we shouldn't be looking at it
and aspiring to it.
And that's something that we need to work
behind the scenes on, you know, doing some modeling
and analysis on what that could look like. And
We've been pretty open, Steve, we've had self
and regional council using that example, NPI
and that over the winter periods coming on
assessing what we are doing.
'cause we wanted to absolute
confidence around that animal animal welfare.
But equally they've been bringing back other, other groups
to have a look at some of those practices
and things like that, that the
work the team are doing down there.
They're pretty proud around how they are managing across
that board number of environmental, animal welfare,
productivity drivers that we're all trying
to balance up down there.
And just to acknowledge it's not a
problem peculiar to you guys.
I mean, we're seeing issues with animals
and mud through cell phone.
That's a, it's a problem.
A broader issue than sort your, I mean,
Right. We've come to
time. So want to thank, um, the team from PMU
for their presentation and the, um, answers.
And I'm sure there'll be probably follow up questions from
the committee via the written, uh,
questions, uh, after this.
So thanks very much your time. Thank you. Thanks all.
Thanks guys. Thank you. Thank you very much.
Advice to help a lot.
